exercises.title: Why games are RL's favorite benchmark
exercises.prompt: List the properties that make games ideal for RL (clean reward, infinite episodes, perfect simulation, etc.), and state the unifying lens the lesson uses to connect AlphaZero, MuZero, and GRPO.
exercises.hint: Win/loss reward, self-play resets, the game IS the simulator, small/discrete actions, adversarial multi-agent structure. Unifying lens: self-play + search + policy improvement.
exercises.solution:
**Why games are ideal for RL:**
- **Clean reward:** unambiguous **win/loss** (or score) — no need to hand-design or learn a reward function; the objective is given.
- **Infinite episodes / free data:** **self-play resets** instantly and infinitely — unlimited training data at no cost or risk.
- **Perfect simulation:** the **game *is* the simulator** — zero reality gap, perfectly reproducible, deterministic rules (for board games).
- **Small / discrete (or modest continuous) action spaces:** tractable to search and optimize over.
- **Multi-agent / adversarial structure:** opponents force **robust, unexploitable** strategies (Lesson 10), not brittle ones.
These are exactly the conditions RL wants, which is why **every major RL breakthrough was tested on games** — TD-Gammon, Atari-DQN, AlphaGo, AlphaZero, OpenAI Five, AlphaStar, MuZero, AlphaTensor/AlphaDev, and DeepSeek-R1 (reasoning).

**The unifying lens:** the lesson views the landmark systems through **self-play + search + policy improvement**:
- **self-play** generates an automatic curriculum of ever-stronger opponents/data,
- **search** (MCTS or, for LLMs, sampling many candidate solutions) looks ahead / explores to find better actions than the current policy would pick,
- **policy improvement** distills the search's better decisions back into the network (train the policy/value toward the search results).
Iterate this loop and the policy bootstraps to superhuman. **AlphaZero, MuZero, and GRPO are all instances of this same loop** — each generalizing the previous, with GRPO applying it to LLM reasoning (tokens as actions, verification as the win signal).

exercises.title: AlphaZero and MuZero
exercises.prompt: Explain AlphaZero's self-play + MCTS + policy/value network loop, and what MuZero changes (a learned model so you don't need the rules). Why does MCTS produce a better policy than the raw network, and why does training the network toward MCTS's output constitute policy improvement?
exercises.hint: AlphaZero: net outputs policy prior + value; MCTS uses them to search; search visit-counts = improved policy; train net toward search results + game outcome; repeat. MuZero learns a latent dynamics model so it can search without knowing transition rules. MCTS > net because lookahead/search refines the prior.
exercises.solution:
**AlphaZero's loop:** a single neural network `f(s) → (p, v)` outputs a **policy prior `p`** (a distribution over moves) and a **value `v`** (estimated win probability). 
1. **Self-play with MCTS:** at each move, run **Monte Carlo Tree Search** guided by the network — MCTS uses `p` to decide which moves to explore (PUCT balances prior, value, and visit counts) and `v` to evaluate leaf nodes, building a search tree and accumulating **visit counts** per move.
2. **The search's visit-count distribution `π`** (which moves MCTS explored most) is an **improved policy** — better than the raw prior `p`.
3. **Play games** to completion using these MCTS policies; record `(state, π, game outcome z)`.
4. **Train the network** to make its **policy prior match the MCTS policy `π`** and its **value match the actual outcome `z`**.
5. **Repeat** — the improved network makes MCTS even stronger next iteration, bootstrapping from random to superhuman, **from scratch, no human games**.

**What MuZero changes:** AlphaZero needs the **rules** (a perfect model: given a state and move, what's the next state?) to run MCTS. **MuZero learns its own *latent* dynamics model** — networks that predict, in an abstract learned state space, the **next latent state, reward, policy, and value** — so it can **run MCTS without being told the rules**. This lets the same algorithm work where the model is unknown (Atari from pixels, and in principle the real world), at the cost of also having to learn the dynamics. It generalizes AlphaZero from 'known-model planning' to 'learned-model planning'.

**Why MCTS beats the raw network:** the network's prior `p` is a **fast, one-shot guess**. MCTS spends **compute at decision time** to **look ahead** — it simulates many move sequences, evaluates resulting positions with `v`, and **backs up** those evaluations (Bellman backups, Lesson 2) to discover that some moves the prior underrated are actually good (and vice versa). This **lookahead refines the prior into a better-informed decision** — search corrects the network's mistakes by actually checking consequences. (It's the value of *test-time compute*: think longer, decide better.)

**Why training toward MCTS = policy improvement:** this is **generalized policy iteration** (Lesson 2/7). MCTS is a **policy-improvement operator**: starting from the policy `p`, it produces a **provably-at-least-as-good policy `π`** (search can only help, given accurate values). **Distilling `π` back into the network** (supervised target) is the **policy-improvement step** — the network now outputs, in one shot, what previously required search. Combined with the **policy-evaluation step** (training `v` toward outcomes), you get the classic improve→evaluate→improve cycle, but with **MCTS as the improvement operator and a neural net as the function approximator**. Each turn of the loop ratchets the policy upward — exactly Bellman's idea, scaled by search + deep learning.

exercises.title: GRPO: AlphaZero's recipe for LLM reasoning
exercises.prompt: DeepSeek-R1 (2025) showed the game-RL recipe works on reasoning, with GRPO replacing PPO. Map the AlphaZero loop onto LLM reasoning (tokens as actions, verification as the win), explain how GRPO's group-relative baseline replaces both the critic and MCTS, why verifiable rewards avoid reward hacking, and what this implies about test-time compute and the future of RL+LLMs.
exercises.hint: State=problem+reasoning so far; action=token; reward=verifier (is the answer correct?). GRPO: sample a GROUP of solutions per problem, reward each, advantage = reward relative to group mean → no value net. 'Search' becomes sampling many chains. Verifiable reward (math/code) can't be gamed like an RM. Test-time compute = longer chains / more samples.
exercises.solution:
**Mapping AlphaZero → LLM reasoning (the MDP of Lesson 1):**
- **State** = the problem statement + reasoning generated so far; **action** = the next **token**; transitions are deterministic (append token).
- **'Win' signal** = a **verifier**: for math, *is the final answer correct?*; for code, *do the tests pass?* This is the clean win/loss reward that makes games great — now available for reasoning because **correctness is checkable**.
- **Self-play / data generation** = the model **generates many reasoning chains** for each problem (its own attempts), analogous to self-play producing training games.
- **Policy improvement** = reinforce the reasoning chains that **reached correct answers**, distilling 'what worked' back into the policy — the same improve loop.

**How GRPO replaces both the critic and MCTS:**
- **Replaces the critic (vs PPO):** PPO needs a learned **value network** as a baseline for advantages (Lessons 7-8) — expensive and unstable. **GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization)** instead **samples a *group* of G solutions to the same problem**, scores each with the verifier, and uses the **group's mean reward as the baseline**: each solution's advantage = its reward **relative to its group-mates** (often normalized by the group's std). This gives the variance-reducing baseline **without any value network** — the group *is* the baseline. (Keeps PPO-style clipped updates + a KL penalty to the reference model.)
- **Replaces MCTS 'search':** AlphaZero searched a game tree at decision time. For LLMs, the analog of search is **sampling many candidate chains and selecting/reinforcing the ones that verify correct** — exploration happens by **drawing a diverse group of solutions** rather than expanding a tree. 'Search' becomes 'sample widely, keep what's verified.' So GRPO is **self-play + (sampling-as-search) + policy improvement** — AlphaZero's loop with tokens as moves and a verifier as the referee.

**Why verifiable rewards avoid reward hacking:** RLHF's reward model is a **learned, gameable proxy** — optimize it hard and the policy finds its blind spots (sycophancy, verbosity; Lessons 1/9). A **verifier for math/code is an *objective, ground-truth* check**: the answer is either right or the tests pass or they don't. There's **no proxy gap to exploit** — the model can only get reward by *actually being correct*, so the usual Goodhart reward-hacking largely disappears. This is why reasoning RL with verifiable rewards is so powerful and stable: the win signal **can't be faked** (within the verifiable domain). (Caveat: it only applies where you *can* verify — math, code, formal tasks; open-ended helpfulness still needs RMs/preferences.)

**Implications for test-time compute and the future:**
- **Test-time compute becomes a first-class lever.** Just as MCTS spent compute at decision time to decide better, reasoning models spend compute by **generating longer chains-of-thought and/or sampling more candidates** ('think longer, answer better'). RL *trains the model to use that test-time compute well* — producing long, self-correcting reasoning. This reframes scaling: not just bigger models/more pretraining (Lesson 7.13's data wall), but **more inference-time search/reasoning**, a new scaling axis.
- **The convergence of the whole phase:** DeepSeek-R1 demonstrates that the **exact game-RL recipe** (self-play + search + policy improvement, Bellman backups + neural function approximation + RL optimization) **transfers from Go to mathematical reasoning** — games were the benchmark, but the techniques are general. The future direction: **RL against verifiable/automatically-checkable rewards** for an expanding set of domains (code, math, tool-use, agentic tasks), **self-improvement loops** where models generate and verify their own training data (Lesson 10's self-play, beyond the human-data ceiling), and **search/test-time-compute** as a core capability. The unsolved frontier is extending verifiable-reward RL to **fuzzy, open-ended objectives** without reintroducing reward hacking — but the trajectory from TD-Gammon (1992) to R1 (2025) shows the same small set of ideas, refined, keeps driving the field's biggest leaps.
